*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In Georgia, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences, with some exceptions.¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility In 2019, 64 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 34 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 14 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 60 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 94 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 68 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 32 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for robbery (20 percent) and aggravated or simple assault (20 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2019, 59 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 9 months when the most serious offense is non-violent and 7 yearsfor people with offenses of more than 21 years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was longer. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Matthew Jacobs, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Georgia *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, July 2022), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/georgia_doi_report_7_7_22.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/georgia_doi_report_7_7_22.pdf).

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43.

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.Georgia Department of Corrections, Daily Population by Facility Calendar Year 2023 (Atlanta, GA: Georgia Department of Corrections, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://gdc.georgia.gov/document/annual-statistical-reports/daily-population-facility-cy-2023/download. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 17 percent, changing from 56,432 in 2010 to 47,010 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 60 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2020, 93 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 17 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, 41 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 16 percent, dropping from 21,717 in 2010 to 18,298 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In 2020, 83 percent of parole-eligible people released in Georgia were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 11 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2019, 42 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 52 percent of people released from prison were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 88 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 37 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 28 percent of people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Parole Release ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_black_Georgia.png){width=1172} ```{=html} In 2019, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Georgia. ``` ### Sex ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_male_Georgia.png){width=1182} ```{=html} In 2019, males were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.4 times higher than females, when accounting for prison population sizes in Georgia.
``` :::

In 2019, Black people who were still incarcerated spent on average 0.7 more years past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people who were still incarcerated spent on average 0.6 more years past parole eligibility compared to White people.

## Notes on Disparities Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of "Other race(s)" may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results. The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate. State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people. ::: ::: {.cell} :::
